Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually setting aside time in the mornings and some evenings throughout the week. With 17 online courses to manage, I had to be somewhat systematic about it, even though I'll admit I didn't always stick to the plan perfectly. I'd typically read through materials and take notes in a structured way since that's how I learn best. My routine was about getting through what needed to be done, checking boxes in a sense, though I know my assignment completion wasn't as strong as my exam performance.
Q2: I aimed for regular times, but honestly, deadlines often disrupted that. When multiple courses had things due around the same time, my schedule would shift dramatically. I'd try to maintain my morning reading sessions, but evenings became unpredictable depending on what was coming up. The consistency I wanted was more of an ideal than a reality most weeks.
Q3: Usually it was checking if there were new announcements or upcoming deadlines that prompted me to log in. I made it a habit to check the LMS fairly regularly—around 14 times per course period—just to stay on top of things. Sometimes I'd log in specifically to participate in discussions since I knew that mattered for my engagement. Other times it was just anxiety about missing something important.
Q4: Yes, logging in became almost automatic for me, especially first thing in the morning with my coffee. After a few weeks, it was just part of my daily routine like checking email. I think the high volume of courses I was taking forced me to build that habit. It wasn't always productive time, but the checking-in part became ingrained.
Q5: The reading materials and written resources were definitely most useful for me since I'm a read-write learner. I found the discussion boards surprisingly helpful too—interacting with peers helped clarify concepts I was struggling with. The videos were good for supplementing, and I watched quite a bit of them, but I retained information better when I could read and take notes at my own pace.
Q6: I often skipped or minimized time on activities that felt redundant or didn't directly connect to exams. Some optional quizzes or practice exercises I'd delay until I felt I absolutely needed them. Honestly, with 17 courses, I had to make choices about where to invest my limited time, and some resources just fell by the wayside even when they might have been valuable.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could see a direct connection to exam preparation or if it helped me understand a difficult concept. Peer discussions were valuable because they gave me different perspectives and helped me feel less isolated. If something felt like busy work without clear learning outcomes, I struggled to prioritize it, especially when I was already feeling stretched thin.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a luxury I couldn't quite afford. Required ones had a sense of urgency that made me engage even when I was tired or stressed. With optional stuff, I'd think 'I'll get to it later' and then later never came. There was guilt about that, but I had to be realistic about what I could handle across all my courses.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings, print some out, and study from my notes away from the computer. I have notebooks full of handwritten summaries that the LMS would never capture. Sometimes I'd study at a café without internet just to focus better, working through problems on paper.
Q10: My exam preparation was largely invisible to the LMS. I'd spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes, creating summary sheets, and testing myself using flashcards I made. Those intense study sessions before exams—where I really solidified my understanding—happened completely offline. The LMS would just show I hadn't logged in, but I was actually deeply engaged with the material.
Q11: Yes, constantly. I'd watch a video once, take detailed notes, and then review those notes multiple times without ever rewatching. Same with readings—I'd access them once to download or print, then study from the physical copy repeatedly. My actual engagement with materials was much deeper than my access patterns would suggest.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, particularly mid-semester when everything piled up at once. I'd feel this wave of sadness and overwhelm, especially when my assignment scores weren't reflecting the effort I felt I was putting in. The CBT sessions I attended helped me work through some of that, but there were weeks where just logging in felt like a monumental task.
Q13: The workload across 17 courses was genuinely overwhelming at times. When I was fatigued, I'd log into the LMS but just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities—work, personal life—meant some weeks I had to triage ruthlessly. The LMS engagement might look consistent, but the quality of that engagement varied wildly based on my stress levels.
Q14: Many times, honestly. I'd have the videos playing while my mind wandered somewhere else entirely. Or I'd scroll through discussion posts without really processing what people were saying. Those sessions counted as 'activity' but I wasn't really learning. It was going through the motions because I felt I should be doing something.
Q15: I postponed assignments more than I should have, which is probably why my assignment completion rate was so low despite my exam scores being high. There was this perfectionism mixed with avoidance—I'd rather study for exams where I felt confident than tackle assignments where I felt uncertain. I knew it was self-sabotaging but breaking that pattern was hard.
Q16: Deadlines were the primary driver of my LMS activity spikes. I'd be relatively steady in my checking and reading, but submission deadlines created these intense bursts of activity. Sometimes I'd cram discussion posts right before cutoffs. The deadline pressure was motivating but also exhausting—it kept me compliant with the routine but not always meaningfully engaged.
Q17: Feedback had a complicated effect on me. Positive feedback on exams reinforced that my study methods were working. But lower assignment scores sometimes made me feel discouraged and less motivated to engage with similar tasks. I'd sometimes avoid checking feedback for a while because I wasn't sure I could handle disappointment in that moment.
Q18: The course structure gave me a framework to follow, which I needed with so many courses running simultaneously. Clear modules and deadlines helped me stay organized. But sometimes the structure felt rigid—like I was just following a checklist rather than exploring ideas that genuinely interested me. The routine became comfortable but maybe too comfortable.
Q19: Having readings and materials clearly organized made engagement much easier for me as a read-write learner. The discussion board format worked well for my learning style. What made it harder was when courses had unclear expectations or when the workload wasn't evenly distributed across weeks. Sudden heavy weeks threw off my careful routine.
Q20: I think that pattern might reflect students who are trying to learn but struggling to translate that into completed work. Maybe they're engaging with materials, participating in discussions, but hitting walls when it comes to actually submitting assignments. It could be perfectionism, confusion about requirements, or just running out of time before deadlines. The effort is there, the output isn't.
Q21: That resonates with me somewhat. My exam scores were strong despite my attendance and assignment completion being lower than they probably should have been. I think some students—myself included—find efficient ways to learn that don't require constant LMS presence. We might study intensively offline and only appear for what's absolutely necessary online.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, how often you check in, what resources you access. But they miss the quality and depth of engagement entirely. They can't see me sitting with my notes for three hours, really wrestling with a concept. They show I watched a video but not whether I understood it or was just letting it play. The numbers are surface-level.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions, making connections between ideas. For me, engagement happens most intensely when I'm alone with my notes, working through problems until they click. It's internal and intellectual, not just behavioral or visible.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the emotional journey—the sadness I've been working through, how the CBT sessions helped me stay functional when I wanted to give up. It wouldn't capture the hours of offline study that actually prepared me for exams. And it definitely wouldn't show the peer conversations outside the platform that helped me feel connected and motivated to continue.
